note:
这个博客最初出现在我的每周时事通讯上，在那里我分享我正在反思的想法，我正在尝试的实验和我学到的教训，所有这些都是为了帮助你提升自己的生活水平。要将这样的帖子直接发送到您的收件箱，请在此处订阅。
YouTube向我推荐了埃隆·马斯克(Elon Musk)如果今天22岁的话会做什么。这段视频有近300万的点击量。在过去，我会立即看完整段视频，受到埃隆认为对人类未来很重要的行业的启发，然后花大量时间研究这一点，并告诉每个人。这一切都是因为埃隆·马斯克认为这很重要。
这是在外包你的思维。这个技巧在高中和大学期间对我很有帮助，但对于长期的成功和幸福来说，它是不可持续的。
外包你的思维有两个问题。继续以《Past Me》为例，有一些事情我已经感兴趣了，对此我有具体的了解，或者在其中拥有竞争优势，这些都不会在埃隆的清单上提及。因此，第一个问题是，我会轻视那些没有意义和重要性的事情，不去追求它们，尽管我在这些方面更适合，更有可能成功。
外包你的思维的第二个问题是，一旦问题的新颖性消失，我将面临导航困难、反对者和创造新事物的摩擦，没有内在的指南针引导我走上正确的道路。简而言之，埃隆·马斯克不是在那里告诉我他认为前进的道路是什么。这一切都源于我追求某件事的问题，不是因为我对那件事感兴趣，实际上喜欢它或认为它很重要，而是因为埃隆·马斯克(或其他任何人)认为它很重要。我跟随他的想法，而不是为自己着想。
这一点很重要的原因是，大多数商业上的成功都是产品-市场-创始人的匹配，而不仅仅是致力于世界变化或热门的东西。这是关于创造一种产品，为正确的市场解决紧迫的问题，并成为正确的人，拥有正确的直觉，能够将产品推向市场，运营公司，取悦这些客户。即使你正在处理一个重要的问题，如果你没有来自你自己的心理模型的信念，当混乱来临时，你也会被灼伤。问问所有2016/17年度的密码专家就知道了。最好是打造一些能表达自己的东西，而不是别人认为聪明的东西。
将你的思维外包是信任他人多于相信自己的错误的表现。乔什·韦茨金(Josh Waitzkin)讲述了这种将你的思维外包的现象是如何在投资界无时无刻不发生的：
“如果你拿投资者来说，可能会有投资，我们认为外部的投资客观上是好的。但从客观上讲，这真的不是件好事。它必须以一种从自己的思维模式中涌现出来的方式，融入自己的投资组合。否则，它就不是自我表现的一种形式。然后，当你进入波动性时，你就不知道该怎么处理它了。“--乔什·维茨金(Josh Waitzkin)
埃隆·马斯克(Elon Musk)是任何人告诉你应该做什么或想什么的人的占位符。他们可能是你崇拜的企业家或风投，也可能是你的父母(尤其是如果你是棕色皮肤的人)。现实是，你不应该在意埃隆·马斯克或其他任何人说什么是重要的，你应该根据自己的好奇心和兴趣，自己决定应该做什么，做自己的实验、探索和思考的艰苦工作。不要把你的想法外包出去。
note_en:
This blog originally appeared in my   weekly newsletter , where I share ideas I’m reflecting upon, experiments I’m trying and lessons I’ve learned, all to help you level up your own life. To get posts like this straight to your inbox,   subscribe here .
  Youtube recommended to me  what Elon Musk would work on if he was 22 years old today. The video has almost 3 million views. In the past, I would’ve immediately watched the whole video, gotten inspired by what Elon thought were industries important to the future of humanity and then spent a ton of time working on that and telling everyone about it. All because Elon Musk thought it was important.
 This is outsourcing your thinking. This trick served me well in highschool and throughout college, but it’s not sustainable for long term success and happiness.
 There are two problems with outsourcing your thinking. Continuing with the example of past me, there are things I’m already interested in, have  specific knowledge about or possess a competitive advantage in, that won’t be mentioned on Elon’s list. Consequently, the first problem is that I will look down on those things as less meaningful and important and not pursue them, despite my better suitability and chances of success in those areas.
 The second problem with outsourcing your thinking is that once the novelty of the problem fades away, I’d be faced with navigating difficulty, naysayers and the friction of creating something new, without an internal compass to guide me toward the correct paths to take. Put simply, Elon Musk isn’t there to talk me through what he thinks the path forward to be. This all stems from the issue that I pursued something, not because I had interest in that thing, actually enjoyed it or thought it was important, but because Elon Musk (or whoever else) thought it was important to work on. And I followed his thinking, rather than thinking for myself.
 The reason this is important is because most success in business is having  product-market-founder fit, not just about working on what’s world changing or hot. It’s about building a product that solves a burning problem for the right market and  being the right person, with the right intuition to bring that product to market, operate that company and delight those customers. Even if you’re working on an important problem, if you don’t have conviction that comes from your own mental models, you’ll get burned when chaos hits. Just ask all the crypto ‘experts’ of 2016/17.  It’s better to build something that’s an expression of yourself, rather than something others think is smart.
 Outsourcing your thinking is a manifestation of the error of trusting others more than we trust ourselves.  Josh Waitzkin talks about how this phenomenon of outsourcing your thinking happens all the time in the investing world:
 “ If you take investors, there might be an investment, which one from the outside we think is objectively good. But it really isn't objectively good. It has to fit into one's portfolio of investments in a way that emerges from one's own mental models. Otherwise, it is not a form of self expression. Then, when you enter volatility, you're not gonna know what to do with it.” -  Josh Waitzkin
 Elon Musk is a placeholder for anyone telling you what you should do or think. That could be entrepreneurs or VCs you idolize or maybe your parents (especially true if you’re brown). The reality is that you should not care what Elon Musk or anyone else says is important, you should decide for yourself what you should work on, based on following your own curiosity and interest.  Do the hard work of experimenting, exploring and thinking for yourself. Don’t outsource your thinking.
title: 我不在乎埃隆·马斯克怎么想了
title_en: I don't care what Elon Musk thinks anymore

note:
丹佛和芝加哥的选民都压倒性地支持当地社区宽带项目，加入了数百个美国社区的行列，这些社区接受了本土的替代方案，以取代根深蒂固的电信垄断。
在芝加哥，大约90%的选民赞成一项不具约束力的公投问题，该问题是：“芝加哥市是否应该采取行动，确保该市所有社区都能接入宽带互联网？投票结果为该市打开了一扇大门，让该市将宽带更多地视为一种基本公用事业，可能是以社区运营的光纤网络的形式对待。”
在丹佛，83.5%的该市选民投票支持问题2H，该问题询问该市是否应该免受2005年一项法律的约束，该法律得到了当地电信垄断的支持，限制科罗拉多州的城镇能够建设自己的本地宽带替代品。
科罗拉多州是通过法律的近20多个州之一，这些法律通常由地区性电信垄断企业直接制定，这些法律阻碍或阻止了此类网络的创建。
但在科罗拉多州的案例中，该州2005年的法律包含了允许当地城镇在选民同意的情况下选择退出限制的措辞。
克里斯托弗·米切尔(Christopher Mitchell)是地方自力更生研究所(Institute for Local Self-Reliance)的社区宽带网络主管，该研究所是一个非营利性组织，倡导可持续发展的地方解决方案。他告诉Motherboard，自科罗拉多州的法律通过以来的15年里，已经有140个社区选择退出，这为朗蒙特的NextLight这样的公民建立的互联网服务提供商打开了大门。
米切尔说：“我认为芝加哥和丹佛的利润率非常高。”“当我们与一半居民垄断有线电视，而另一半居民没有宽带的社区合作时，这两个群体对更好的东西的需求都很强烈。”
研究表明，4200万美国人无法使用任何宽带，这是FCC官方估计的两倍。米切尔的组织估计，大约还有8300万美国人生活在宽带垄断之下，通常是康卡斯特(Comcast)。还有数千万人生活在双头垄断之下，通常由康卡斯特(Comcast)和基本上冷漠的地区电信公司组成，销售老化的DSL线路。
由于竞争减弱，监管机构和立法者基本上忠于根深蒂固的垄断，最终结果是市场破裂，美国消费者为缓慢的宽带和糟糕的客户服务支付了发达国家中一些最高的价格。
米切尔说：“多年来，我们一直说这是选民的主要担忧，但地方领导人仍然被大型垄断有线电视和电话公司吓坏了，没有采取行动。”“也许现在我们会看到这件事得到更严肃的对待。”
哈佛大学的一项研究发现，基于社区的宽带替代方案通常比大多数地区垄断企业提供更快的速度、更低的价格和更好的客户服务。这样的网络也会促使现有的互联网服务提供商在缺乏竞争的情况下安心地部署升级和降价，否则这些升级和降价是不会实现的。
米切尔的团队认为，虽然社区支持的宽带不是灵丹妙药，但这类网络也倾向于对当地的投诉做出更好的回应，因为它们是由当地居民拥有和运营的，他们对社区的成功有着既得利益。
米切尔说：“如果我们在丹佛和芝加哥的组织者所做的基础上，没有在全国各地的社区看到更多的非约束性公投活动，我会感到惊讶。”
note_en:
Voters in both Denver and Chicago have overwhelmingly thrown their support behind local community broadband projects, joining the  hundreds of U.S. communities that have embraced home-grown alternatives to entrenched telecom monopolies.
  In Chicago, roughly  90 percent of voters approved a non-binding referendum question that asked: “should the city of Chicago act to ensure that all the city's community areas have access to broadband Internet?" The vote opens the door to the city treating broadband more like an essential utility, potentially in the form of community-run fiber networks.
   In Denver, 83.5 percent of the city’s electorate cast ballots in favor of  question 2H, which asked if the city should be exempt from a 2005 law, backed by local telecom monopolies, restricting Colorado towns and cities from being able to build their own local broadband alternatives.
  Colorado is one of  nearly two-dozen states that have passed laws, usually directly  written by regional telecom monopolies, that hamstring or prevent the creation of such networks.
  But in Colorado’s case, the state’s 2005 law included language that allows local towns and cities to opt-out of the restriction if voters agree to do so.
   Christopher Mitchell is director of community broadband networks for the Institute for Local Self-Reliance, a nonprofit that advocates for local solutions for sustainable development. He told Motherboard that in the 15 years since Colorado’s law was passed, 140 communities have opted out, opening the door to citizen-built ISPs like  Nextlight in Longmont.
   “I think the margin in Chicago and Denver is remarkable,” Mitchell said. “When we work with communities where half the residents have a cable monopoly and the other half don't have any broadband, the demand for something better is strong among both populations.”
  Studies suggest that  42 million Americans lack access to any broadband whatsoever, double official FCC estimates. Mitchell’s organization estimates that roughly  83 million more Americans live under a broadband monopoly, usually Comcast. Tens of millions more live under a duopoly, usually consisting of Comcast and a  largely apathetic regional telco selling aging DSL lines.
   With muted competition and regulators and lawmakers largely loyal to entrenched monopolies, the end result is a broken market in which U.S. consumers pay some of the highest prices in the developed world for slow broadband and  abysmal customer service.
  “For years, we have said this is a major concern for voters but local leaders remain too intimidated by the big monopoly cable and telephone companies to act on it,” Mitchell said. “Maybe now we will see it taken more seriously.”
  A  Harvard study found that community-based broadband alternatives routinely offer faster speeds, lower prices, and better customer service than most regional monopolies. Such networks can also drive incumbent ISPs, all too comfortable with the lack of competition, to deploy upgrades and price reductions that  wouldn’t have materialized otherwise.
  Mitchell’s group argues that while community-backed broadband isn’t a silver bullet, such networks also tend to be more responsive to local complaints because they’re owned and operated by local residents with a vested interest in the success of their neighborhoods.
   “I will be surprised if we don't see more campaigns for non-binding referenda in communities around the country that build on what organizers in Denver and Chicago have done,” Mitchell said.
title: 芝加哥和丹佛选民压倒性支持社区宽带
title_en: Voters Overwhelmingly Back Community Broadband in Chicago and Denver

note:
生活在美国空气污染严重的地区，死于新冠肺炎的风险要高得多，这增加了空气污染数据被用来预测哪些地区可能最需要帮助治疗这种疾病的可能性。
由于长期暴露在空气污染中会削弱肺部，新冠肺炎会攻击肺部，世界各地的研究人员一直在争先恐后地确定糟糕的空气质量是否会使这种疾病变得更加严重。已经得出了联系，但许多研究没有考虑到这种联系的其他可能原因，比如人口密度。
哈佛大学的弗朗西丝卡·多明尼奇和她的同事们现在发现，从长远来看，每立方米空气中每增加一微克微小颗粒物(PM2.5)，新冠肺炎的死亡率就会增加11%。这使得新冠肺炎与空气污染之间的联系大致等同于疾病与吸烟之间的联系。
多明尼奇说：“这是第一项提供了一些一致证据的研究，证明如果你生活在一个细颗粒物水平较高的[美国]县，它会增加家禽死亡的风险。”她的团队检查了新冠肺炎截至6月18日的美国3089个县的死亡数据，并对2000年至2016年的PM2.5水平进行了建模，精确到县级。
重要的是，即使在调整了包括吸烟、财富、年龄和种族在内的20个其他可能原因的结果后，空气污染和更高的死亡率之间的联系也是显而易见的。然而，多明尼奇说，分析的一个很大局限性是，关于死亡、污染和其他潜在原因的数据是在地区层面上的，而不是在个人层面上，这阻碍了分析的准确性。不过，她说，这仍然是测量链接的最佳方式，直到研究人员在一年左右的时间内可以获得正在被捕获的个人级别的数据。
虽然没有办法消除长期空气污染造成的危害，但多米尼克表示，找到新冠肺炎死亡的原因“意义重大”。例如，遭受空气污染的地区可以优先获得更多的医院床位或个人防护用品(PPE)。由于新冠肺炎可能会和我们在一起多年，现在努力制造空气净化器可能会帮助人们在未来应对这种疾病。
伦敦玛丽女王大学(Queen Mary University Of London)的乔纳森·格里格(Jonathan Grigg)没有参与这项研究，他说这是一项很好的研究，与其他新出现的证据一致。“(污染暴露)相对较小的变化完全有可能与(新冠肺炎死亡率)风险增加相关。”这是有道理的。“他说。
英国心脏基金会和英国爱丁堡大学的马克·米勒(Mark Miller)也没有参与这项研究，他说，我们现在需要更好地了解肮脏空气和新冠肺炎死亡率之间联系的生物学基础。“这些发现可能仅仅是因为空气污染和新冠肺炎都影响了同样的弱势群体--老年人和患有呼吸系统或心血管疾病的人--还是有别的原因？”
note_en:
Living in a part of the United States with dirty air has been linked to a significantly greater risk of dying from covid-19, raising the prospect of air pollution data being used to forecast which areas may need the most help treating people with the illness.
 As long-term exposure to air pollution weakens the lungs, and covid-19 attacks them, researchers worldwide have been racing to establish whether poor air quality makes the disease more severe. Links have been drawn, but  many studies fail to account for other possible reasons for the associations, such as population density.
 Francesca Dominici at Harvard University and her colleagues have now found that each extra microgram of tiny particulate matter – PM2.5 – per cubic metre of air over the long term increases the covid-19 mortality rate by 11 per cent. That puts the link between covid-19 and air pollution roughly on a par with the link between the disease and smoking.
   “This is the first study that provides some consistent evidence that, if you’re living in a [US] county with a higher level of fine particulate matter, it increases the risk of covid mortality,” says Dominici. Her team examined covid-19 death data up to 18 June for 3089 US counties, and modelled PM2.5 levels for 2000 to 2016 down to county level.
  Importantly, the link between air pollution and higher death rates was clear even after adjusting results for 20 other possible explanations, including smoking, wealth, age and race. However, Dominici says a big limitation of the analysis is that the data on deaths, pollution and the other potential reasons is at an area level, rather than the level of individuals, hindering its accuracy. Still, she says it remains the best way to measure links until individual-level data, which is being captured, becomes available to researchers in a year or so.
 While there is no way to undo the harm that long-term air pollution inflicts, Dominici says finding the link with covid-19 deaths “matters a tonne”. For example, areas that have suffered from dirty air could be prioritised for more hospital beds or PPE. And with covid-19 likely to be with us for years, efforts to make air cleaner now could help people cope with the illness in future.
 Jonathan Grigg at Queen Mary University of London, who wasn’t involved in the study, says it is good research and in line with other emerging evidence. “It’s entirely possible that relatively small changes [in pollution exposure] are going to be associated with increased risk [in covid-19 mortality rates]. It’s plausible,” he says.
 Mark Miller at the British Heart Foundation and University of Edinburgh, UK, who also wasn’t involved in the research, says we now need to better understand the biology underlying the link between dirty air and covid-19 mortality rates. “Could these findings simply be because both air pollution and covid-19 affect the same vulnerable groups – the elderly and those with respiratory or cardiovascular disease – or is there something more going on?”
title: 空气污染与美国更大的新冠肺炎死亡风险有关
title_en: Air pollution linked to greater risk of dying from Covid-19 in the US

note:
对于某一类工人来说，这场大流行代表着时空职业连续体的断裂。(工业和信息化部电子科学技术情报研究所陈皓)。许多美国人被困住了，被孩子们束缚住了，或者失去了收入，或者承担了照顾病人的义务。但对于那些无忧无虑、在任何地方都能找到稳定工作的人来说，这是一个抓住命运、让就业向自己倾斜的时刻。
他们的逻辑既令人羡慕，也让其他人望而却步：如果你打算无限期地在家工作，为什么不在一个充满异国情调的地方建一个新家呢？这一小群人收集了他们的MacBook、护照和N95面具，成为了数字游牧民。
他们在巴厘岛空荡荡的海滩度假胜地发Instagram，在精心设计的露营面包车上参加Zoom会议。他们在廉价的Tulum Airbnbs建造了阳台办公室，并用Wi-Fi预订了州立公园的露营地。他们实际上是申请加勒比海国家大肆宣传的远程工人签证项目的那类人。偶尔，他们会泄气。
洛杉矶企业家大卫·马尔卡(David Malka)收到了朋友们的来信，他们在国外过着最好的工作生活。今年6月，他制定了一个计划：他和女朋友将在阿姆斯特丹工作，沿途在墨西哥的一个折扣度假胜地短暂停留。
第一个障碍几乎立刻就发生了。在圣卢卡斯角，马尔卡和他的女朋友意识到，欧盟不会像他们希望的那样重新向美国游客开放边境。回到美国是不可能的：马尔卡的女朋友来自英国，她的签证不允许这样做。
两人决定在墨西哥多呆一段时间。马尔卡说，起初它很迷人。在笔记本电脑上工作--他管理着一系列度假租赁物业--他们有了自己的度假胜地。但到了第二周，他们的处境开始感觉像“土拨鼠节”。城市和海滩都关闭了，所以这对夫妇从未离开过度假村。与此同时，旅行停摆正在重创他的业务。
马尔卡说，“我们能做的就是坐在游泳池边，或者去健身房。”重复、无聊和孤立都让他们疲惫不堪。
最终，这对夫妇在阿姆斯特丹停留了28个小时，中途停留了两次。在那里，马尔卡确实被海关拒之门外。他们撤退到伦敦，在那里他们很快分手了。
从那时起，他就一直在那里。“寒冷，下雨，令人沮丧，”他说。“这是人们首先想到的三个形容词。”
现在马尔卡正在努力想办法去巴厘岛--严格来说，巴厘岛不对游客开放，但他听说有一种特殊签证，可以抢购800美元--或者利用他的血统获得葡萄牙公民身份。这需要大量的后勤工作。
马尔卡远不是唯一一个在路上跌跌撞撞的科维德游牧民。事实证明，潮流故事和Instagram上的帖子没有分享一些缺点。交税。繁文缛节的事情。Wi-Fi狂热的东西。关闭边境的事情。在做出一个情绪激动、隔离混乱的决定时，人们可能会掩盖这些事情：收拾好一套公寓，订一张去巴拿马、蒙特利尔或加德满都的单程票。
美国人从来都不擅长度假。在新冠肺炎之前，他们有数以亿计的带薪休息日闲置。他们甚至创造了工作和度假的混合体--工作。这个想法是：去一个好地方旅行，白天工作，然后在理论上享受下班时间的风景。在大流行时期，数字游牧民只是把工作变成了一种永久的状态。
坏消息是，这对两个世界都是最糟糕的。他们应该在新的、美丽的环境中尽情享受。但他们不能尽情享受，因为工作在召唤他们。焦虑的自我优化乒乓球之间的“为什么我没有过上我最好的生活？”还有“我为什么不在工作中尽情享受呢？”
凯蒂·史密斯·阿代尔(Katie Smith-Adair)和她的丈夫经营着加州洛斯阿拉莫斯的PlaceInvaders活动公司。当大流行停止营业时，他们带着帐篷和户外淋浴收拾了他们的沃尔沃，准备在西部进行为期一个月的露营公路旅行。在此期间，她每周工作40个小时，试图为虚拟活动设置PlaceInvaders。
学到的第一课是什么？永远不要相信露营地的Wi-Fi。第二个呢？预计营地工作人员会因为需要Wi-Fi而做出判断。
史密斯-阿代尔说：“它们会让你感觉很糟糕，因为你不会拔掉电源，融入大自然。”他说：“这是我的工作。我想拔掉插头，但我也必须尽快接上Zoom的电话。“。在爱达荷州博伊西附近的一个房车公园，她注意到一个Wi-Fi热点，它的名字相当于对所有加州人的中指。
史密斯-阿代尔的办公室成了附近麦当劳或星巴克门外人行道上的折叠椅。这并不是一个与红杉和平相处的社区。在俄勒冈州尤金(Eugene)的一次路边电话会议上，附近一名手持除草机的男子开始轰鸣他的马达。阿代尔-史密斯告诉他，她正在努力挽救自己的事业。他不在乎。
当数字游牧民确实设法沉迷于他们新家的华丽时，他们可以体验到另一种更具通灵性的代价：仇恨者。拉斯维加斯的短期租赁运营商奥斯汀·毛(Austin毛)在Facebook上发帖称，他和妻子在3月份逃到了哥斯达黎加，并惊讶地收到了大量愤怒的评论。有人指控他散布Covid病毒。他们对他弃国而去感到愤慨。有一个人甚至取消了他的好友关系。
毛先生说，在仇恨者之后，是一种负罪感。在大流行期间，他和一些朋友通过每月的Zoom电话保持联系。谈话有一个结构：朋友们轮流描述他们生活中哪些事情进展顺利，哪些事情不太顺利，他们称之为“前5%”和“后5%”。在哥斯达黎加，毛先生会分享他吃自己捕到的鱼，和鲸鱼、鲨鱼一起潜水的故事。他住在一座海滨丛林别墅里，猴子会把椰子从树上敲下来，他会用砍刀把椰子砍开，品尝新鲜的椰汁。
他的朋友们正在美国隔离，却没有如此丰富的材料。他们的故事几乎没有改变。“他们经常说，‘我没有前五名或后五名。我的生活有点乏味，’”毛先生说。“感觉就像我在作弊。”
六个月后，毛先生和他的妻子王楚初需要回到美国，让她保留绿卡。他注意到了一个变化：在Facebook上发泄对他的冠状病毒愤怒的朋友们都过来了。现在，毛先生说，他们正在问他，他们如何才能实现类似的逃脱。
也许最糟糕的潜在结果不是那些逃脱的人所宣传的：你和你的雇主可能最终会被关进税务审计地狱。
许多美国旅行者试图使用一项税收规则，为居住在国外的美国人划出免税条款。但这需要一年中整整330天不在国外，这还不包括旅行。如果搞砸了，会受到严厉的惩罚。
“这是间歇性的税收禁食，”WiFi部落(WiFi Tribe)的总法律顾问亚历山大·斯特利亚努迪斯(Alexander Stylianoudis)说。该组织帮助900名数字游牧民方便旅行。“每个人都在谈论这件事，但每个人都做错了。”Stylianoudis说，自疫情爆发以来，他看到的错误数量成倍增加。
一些工人通过简单地忘记向他们的雇主提及他们的位置来避免这一点--也就是“不问不说”的远程疫情工作。其他人一直很诚实，活着就后悔了。一家上市科技公司的一名员工，在3月份她的办公室关闭时去了加拿大。今年9月，在升职两周后，该公司突然告诉她，她必须在两周内返回美国，否则就会辞职。她被告知，这么做的原因是为了避免缴纳外国税。(她要求匿名，因为情况尚未解决。)。
其他雇主正试图决定在哪里划清界限。金融科技创业公司Estimize的首席执行官利·德罗根(Leigh Dgen)说，他拒绝了一名员工的要求，让他在不同国家之间周游一整年，但允许这名员工去西班牙六个月。德根说，Estimize对员工的监督已经很“薄弱”，他担心员工在四处走动时无法集中精力。
还有签证问题。在过去的几年里，数字游牧民会根据需要来回穿越边境，以避免逾期居留。在一场封闭的边境大流行中，这并不容易。
今年3月，商业顾问瑞安·麦考姆(Ryan McCumber)被困在葡萄牙。他当时正在欧洲旅行，一场充满错误的喜剧和突如其来的全洲封锁让他滞留在海滨小镇阿尔加维，身上只有四天的衣服，而他的狗和其他行李仍留在上一站华沙。
疫情使他的会议业务无法生存，因此在葡萄牙期间，他决定创建一个专注于体育技术的创业加速器。麦考姆说，最大的挑战是，当他在海滩上接电话时，不要让他在美国的伙伴太嫉妒。
虽然一名抢劫犯袭击了他，给他缝了15针，眼睛上方有一道伤疤，但他爱上了葡萄牙廉价的桑格里亚酒和海洋空气。初夏，当他的航空公司最终提供他回家的航班时，他不想离开。由于签证已经过期，麦康姆前往移民局申请政治庇护。
“我说，‘特朗普是个独裁者，我的城市正在燃烧，人们正在死去，’”他说。他引用了总统的话，抗议警察的暴力行为和病毒。“他们开玩笑说，我是越南战争以来第一个提出这一要求的美国人。”
他说，政府工作人员笑了，然后批准延长到10月底。(麦康姆后来回到了美国。)
其他人也在为马尔卡一样的假期疲惫而苦苦挣扎，马尔卡是从卡波到伦敦再到巴厘岛的流浪者。荷兰拉德布大学进行的一项研究显示，人们需要8天假期才能达到最高幸福感。从那里开始就是下坡路了。
疫情来袭时，律师斯蒂利亚努迪斯正在泰国的潘干岛(Koh Phangan)。起初，他不能抱怨热带的环境。每天下班后，他都会在晶莹剔透的水中游泳。但五个月后，他迫不及待地想离开。他已经成为岛上7-11的常客。他甚至厌倦了海滩--这是他认为不可能的事情。
被困在天堂的感觉很难解释。他说：“我开始觉得自己像是在拍《迷失》的续集。
今年夏天，住在加利福尼亚州洛斯阿尔托斯的风险投资人凯蒂·雅各布斯·斯坦顿(Katie Jacobs Stanton)看到了重新开始的时刻。她即将成为一名空巢老人，有两个孩子在上大学，另一个正在休学一年。她的父亲死于柯维病，独自一人呆在家里，尤其是在加州的野火中，太令人沮丧了。意识到自己可以在任何地方找到风险投资并达成交易，她决定利用自己的间隔年。
今年8月，雅各布斯·斯坦顿(Jacobs Stanton)放弃了大部分财产，买了一辆特斯拉(Tesla)，准备带着她的金毛猎犬塔科(Taco)上路。她说：“在我的脑海中，我看到了一幅美轮美奂的美景，还有一对夫妻店，就是美国的主街(Main Street)。”
她发现了一个不同的现实。首先，有人偷了她的特斯拉。(警方找回了它。)。然后，她的第一站雷诺(Reno)变得冷酷无情。“这是一个非常悲伤的城市，”她说。在塔霍省，野火肆虐。在蒙大拿州的博兹曼，塔可病了。一次去看兽医的旅行导致了紧急手术；塔可吃了一只管袜子。
然后是坎耶·韦斯特。今年9月，雅各布斯·斯坦顿(Jacobs Stanton)与这位嘻哈艺人通了电话，讨论进军音乐行业的可能性，他在Twitter上提到了这一点。她说，查名引发了“我一生中最疯狂的一天”。雅各布斯·斯坦顿收到的信息让她应接不暇，但积极的一面是，这件事促使她的三个孩子都打电话来报到。
她在白雪皑皑的黄石公园露营时冻僵了，对结冰的道路感到紧张。后来，她上大学的女儿感染了冠状病毒，塔可需要做更多的手术。(两人都已康复。)。最重要的是，她走访的小镇上的人们没有戴口罩，而且对此怀有敌意。
到了10月下旬，她已经准备好把自己的间隔年称为短暂的。“我想我只想回家，”她说。“凯蒂·斯坦顿再也不能自驾游了。”
更正式的工作假期正在涌现，特别是在科技行业，该行业催生了Zoom和Slack等工具，在大流行期间让员工搬迁的速度最快。
今年夏天，旧金山Airbnb和优步(Uber)的前产品经理迈克尔·霍克(Michael Houck)邀请了18名企业家到图卢姆一栋租来的墨西哥豪宅工作。他把它叫做“发射屋”(Launch House)。在一个月的时间里，他和船员们上传了他们在海滩上潜水、分享食物和使用笔记本电脑的照片。
10月下旬，当第二批创业者来到这里时，霍克把房子升级成了一栋Wi-Fi更快的房子，还有一名私人厨师、一名管家，泳池里还摆放着白板，用来进行“泳池风暴”--在水中进行头脑风暴。
霍克说，他们的目标是帮助创始人发布新的应用程序和软件。“我们不会去图卢姆狂欢，也不会去俱乐部，”他说。“我们在海滩上工作，在房子里工作。”
当热带风暴和飓风切断电力供应时，他们无法在任何地方工作。“现实地说，在图卢姆这样的地方，Wi-Fi总有可能会断掉，”胡克说。“这是你做出的权衡。”
当然，风险更大的权衡是大流行。第一组中有三个人感染了病毒，胡克说，他们违反了家规，与外界交往过一段时间。第二批企业家被告知，如果他们威胁到集团的安全，他们将被驱逐。
这提醒人们，Covid Carpe Diem套装冒着巨大的风险。这个千载难逢的时刻存在的原因与我们大多数人不能走进办公室、不能真正度假或不能在餐厅用餐的原因是一样的。旅行有生病的危险。只争朝夕有生病的危险。
去年10月，风险投资人布雷特·马丁(Brett Martin)与四个朋友--一名比特币交易员、一名作家、一名企业家和一名建筑师--进行了一次谈心的Zoom通话。在那之前，该组织一直在计划从11月到3月搬到他们所说的哥斯达黎加的“分时公社”。
规则已经制定：他们将通过哥斯达黎加政府购买医疗保险。他们会在中途前往尼加拉瓜进行“签证之旅”，在护照上盖章。禁止在室内用餐。不能在主要生活区工作或召开电话会议。食品杂货会被送到。参观者只能在集体同意的情况下才能参观。
但有一个问题仍然存在。马丁说，“离家很远，在一个你不会说当地语言的地方病得很厉害，感到孤独和无助--这是每个人都害怕的。”
在Zoom Call上，他们决定：一个人都不能掉队。马丁说，“如果有人生病了，这就像一个家庭。”(以防万一，他已经服了两个月的Duolingo。)。
他承认，这种安排很容易变得糟糕。这个团体的成员将不得不适应再次有室友的情况。互联网可能会不复存在。比特币的价格可能会暴跌。他必须在飞行前72小时对冠状病毒检测进行完美的计时，才能得到结果。但他说，经过七个月的隔离，冒这个险是值得的。
“我在纽约的单间公寓里6点起床，锻炼身体，坐在笔记本前10到12个小时，然后合上笔记本，盯着墙，然后再打开看Netflix，整个晚上都在看--”他慢慢地写道，“这一天早上6点起床，锻炼身体，坐在笔记本前10到12个小时，然后合上笔记本，盯着墙，然后再打开看Netflix。”“这听起来对我的健康真的很危险。”
他于11月初抵达哥斯达黎加。每天都下着倾盆大雨。“烈酒，”他说，“仍然很高。”
note_en:
For a certain kind of worker, the pandemic presented a rupture in the space-time-career continuum. Many Americans were stuck, tied down by children or lost income or obligations to take care of the sick. But for those who were unencumbered, with steady jobs that were doable from anywhere, it was a moment to grab destiny and bend employment to their favor.
 Their logic was as enviable as it was unattainable for everyone else: If you’re going to work from home indefinitely, why not make a new home in an exotic place? This tiny cohort gathered their MacBooks, passports and N95 masks and became digital nomads.
 They Instagrammed their workdays from  empty beach resorts in Bali and took Zoom meetings  from tricked-out camper vans. They made balcony offices at cheap  Tulum Airbnbs and booked  state park campsites with Wi-Fi. They were the kind of people who actually applied to those remote worker  visa programs heavily advertised by Caribbean  countries. And occasionally they were deflated.
 David Malka, an entrepreneur in Los Angeles, had heard from friends who were living their best work-abroad lives. In June, he created a plan: He and his girlfriend would work from Amsterdam, with a quick stop at a discounted resort in Mexico along the way.
 The first snag happened almost immediately. In Cabo San Lucas, Mr. Malka and his girlfriend realized that the European Union wasn’t about to reopen its borders to American travelers, as they had hoped. Returning to the United States wasn’t an option: Mr. Malka’s girlfriend was from the United Kingdom, and her visa wouldn’t allow it.
 The two decided to stay in Mexico a bit longer. At first it was glamorous, Mr. Malka said. Working by laptop — he manages a portfolio of vacation rental properties — they had the resort to themselves. But by the second week, their situation began to feel like “Groundhog Day.” The city and the beach were closed, so the couple never left the resort. Meanwhile, the travel shutdown was hammering his business.
 “All we could do is sit by the pool or go to the gym,” Mr. Malka said. The repetition, boredom and isolation all wore on them.
  Eventually, the couple took a 28-hour, two-layover trip to Amsterdam, where Mr. Malka was indeed turned away at customs. They retreated to London, where they promptly broke up.
 He has been there since. “Cold, raining, depressing,” he said. “Those are the first three adjectives that come to mind.”
 Now Mr. Malka is trying to figure out how to get to Bali — it’s technically closed to visitors, but he heard about a special visa that can be rushed for $800 — or use his ancestry to obtain Portuguese citizenship. It’s a lot of logistics.
  Mr. Malka is far from the only Covid nomad to stumble on the road. It turns out there are drawbacks the trend stories and Instagram posts didn’t share. Tax things. Red-tape things. Wi-Fi rage things. Closed border things. The kinds of things one might gloss over when making an emotional, quarantine-addled decision to pack up an apartment and book a one-way ticket to Panama or Montreal or Kathmandu.
  Americans have never been especially good at vacation. Before Covid-19, they were leaving unused  hundreds of millions of paid days off. They even created a work-vacation hybrid — the  workation. The idea: Travel to a nice place, work during the day and then, in theory, enjoy the scenery in the off hours. In pandemic times, the digital nomads have simply made workation a permanent state.
 The bad news is it’s the worst of both worlds. They should be enjoying themselves in their new, beautiful surroundings. But they can’t enjoy themselves, because work beckons. The anxious self-optimization pingpongs between “Why aren’t I living my best life?” and “Why aren’t I killing it at work?”
 Katie Smith-Adair and her husband run PlaceInvaders, an event company in Los Alamos, Calif. When the pandemic halted business, they packed up their Volvo with a tent and an outdoor shower for a monthlong camping road trip around the West. All the while, she worked 40 hours a week trying to set up PlaceInvaders for virtual events.
 The first lesson learned? Never trust campground Wi-Fi. The second? Expect judgment from campground workers for needing the Wi-Fi.
  “They make you feel bad because you’re not unplugging and getting into nature,” Ms. Smith-Adair said. “This is my job. I want to unplug, but I also have to get on that Zoom call real quick.” At an R.V. park near Boise, Idaho, she noticed a Wi-Fi hot spot whose name was the equivalent of a middle finger directed at all Californians.
 Ms. Smith-Adair’s office became a folding chair on the sidewalk outside whatever McDonald’s or Starbucks was nearby. It wasn’t exactly a peaceful commune with the redwoods. During one curbside conference call in Eugene, Ore., a nearby man with a weed whacker began roaring his motor. Ms. Adair-Smith told him that she was trying to salvage her career. He didn’t care.
 When digital nomads do manage to indulge in the splendors of their new homes, they can experience another, more psychic toll: the haters. Austin Mao, a short-term-rental operator in Las Vegas, posted on Facebook about escaping to Costa Rica with his wife in March, and was surprised to receive a flood of angry comments. People accused of him spreading Covid. They were outraged that he had abandoned his country. One person even unfriended him.
 After the haters, Mr. Mao said, came the guilt. During the pandemic, he and some friends have kept in touch via monthly Zoom calls. The conversations have a structure: The friends take turns describing what’s going well and not so well in their lives, which they refer to as their “top 5 percent” and “bottom 5 percent.” In Costa Rica, Mr. Mao would share tales of eating fish he had caught himself and diving with whales and sharks. He was living in a beachfront jungle villa where monkeys would knock coconuts from the trees, and he would chop them open with a machete, savoring the fresh juice.
  His friends, who were quarantining in the United States, had no such wealth of material. Their stories rarely changed. “They would frequently say, ‘I don’t have a top 5 or bottom 5. My life is kind of blasé,’” Mr. Mao said. “It felt like I was cheating.”
 After six months, Mr. Mao and his wife, Chuchu Wang, needed to return to the United States for her to keep her green card. He noticed a change: The Facebook friends who had taken out their coronavirus-rage on him came around. Now, Mr. Mao said, they’re asking him how they can pull off a similar escape.
  Perhaps the worst potential outcome not advertised by those who’ve escaped: You and your employer could end up in tax audit hell.
  Lots of American travelers try to use a tax  rule that carves out exemptions for Americans living abroad. But it requires being out of the country 330 full days of the year, not counting travel. Messing it up brings severe penalties.
 “It’s the intermittent fasting of taxes,” said Alexander Stylianoudis, the general counsel at WiFi Tribe, a group that helps facilitate travel for 900 digital nomads. “Everyone talks about it, and everyone does it wrong.” The number of mistakes he has seen since the pandemic has multiplied, Mr. Stylianoudis said.
 Some workers have avoided that by simply forgetting to mention their location to their employers — the “don’t ask, don’t tell” of remote pandemic work. Others have been honest and lived to regret it. One employee of a publicly traded tech company, went to Canada when her office closed in March. In September, two weeks after a promotion, the company suddenly told her that she had to return to the United States within two weeks or resign. The reason, she was informed, was to avoid paying foreign taxes. (She asked that her name be withheld because the situation is unresolved.)
 Other employers are trying to decide where to draw the line. Leigh Drogen, the chief executive of Estimize, a fintech start-up, said he had discouraged an employee’s request to bounce around from country to country for a full year, but had given permission for the worker to go to Spain for six months. Estimize’s oversight of its staff is already “thin,” Mr. Drogen said, and he worried about the employee’s ability to focus while moving around.
  There are also visa issues. In years past, digital nomads would cross and recross borders as needed to avoid overstaying. That’s not so easy in a closed-border pandemic.
 In March, Ryan McCumber, a business consultant, was stuck in Portugal. He had been traveling in Europe, and a comedy of errors and the sudden continentwide lockdown stranded him in a beach town, Algarve, with just four days of clothes while his dog and the rest of his luggage remained in Warsaw, a previous stop.
 The pandemic made his conference business nonviable, so while in Portugal he decided to create a start-up accelerator focused on sports technology. The biggest challenge, Mr. McCumber said, was not making his partners in the United States too jealous while he took calls from the beach.
 Although a mugger assaulted him, giving him 15 stitches and a scar above his eye, he fell in love with Portugal’s cheap sangrias and ocean air, and in early summer, when his airline finally offered him a flight home, he didn’t want to leave. With his visa already expired, Mr. McCumber went to the immigration bureau and asked for political asylum.
 “I said, ‘Trump’s a dictator, my city is burning, and people are dying,’” he said, citing the president, protests against police violence and the virus. “They made a joke that I was the first person since the Vietnam War from America to ask for that.”
 The government workers laughed, he said, and then approved an extension through the end of October. (Mr. McCumber has since returned to the United States.)
 Others are struggling with the same vacation fatigue experienced by Mr. Malka, the Cabo-to-London-to-maybe-Bali wanderer. According to  research conducted at Radboud University in the Netherlands, it takes eight days of vacation for people to reach peak happiness. It’s downhill from there.
 When the pandemic hit, Mr. Stylianoudis, the lawyer, was on the island of Koh Phangan in Thailand. At first, he couldn’t complain about the tropical locale. Each day, after work, he swam in crystal-clear water. But after five months, he was itching to get out. He had become a regular at the island’s 7-Eleven. He even grew tired of the beach — something he hadn’t thought was possible.
 The feeling of being trapped in paradise was hard to explain. “I started to feel like I was in a sequel of ‘Lost,’” he said.
  This summer, Katie Jacobs Stanton, a venture capital investor living in Los Altos, Calif., saw her moment for a fresh start. She was about to become an empty nester, with two children in college and another taking a gap year. Her father had died of Covid, and being alone at home, especially amid California’s wildfires, was too depressing. Figuring she could find venture investments and strike deals from anywhere, she decided to take a gap year of her own.
 In August, Ms. Jacobs Stanton gave away most of her possessions, bought a Tesla and prepared to hit the open road with Taco, her golden retriever. “I had this image of a glorious, beautiful American landscape and mom-and-pop, Main Street U.S.A.,” she said.
  She found a different reality. First, someone stole her Tesla. (The police recovered it.) Then her first stop, Reno, was grim. “It’s a very sad city,” she said. In Tahoe, wildfires raged. In Bozeman, Mont., Taco became sick. A trip to a veterinarian led to emergency surgery; Taco had eaten a tube sock.
  Then came Kanye West. In September, Ms. Jacobs Stanton had a phone call with the hip-hop artist about a possible venture in the music business, which he  mentioned on Twitter. Name-checking her set off “one of the most bananas days in my life,” she said. Ms. Jacobs Stanton was overwhelmed with messages, but on the plus side, the episode prompted all three of her children to call and check in.
 She froze while camping in snowy Yellowstone and stressed over the icy roads. Then her daughter in college got the coronavirus, and Taco needed more surgery. (Both recovered.) On top of it all, people in small towns she visited didn’t wear masks and were hostile about it.
 By late October, she was ready to call her gap year short. “I think I just want to go back home,” she said. “No more road trips for Katie Stanton.”
  More formal workation getaways are bubbling up, particularly in the tech industry, which spawned tools like Zoom and Slack and has been the fastest to let employees relocate in the pandemic.
 Over the summer, Michael Houck, a former product manager at Airbnb and Uber in San Francisco, invited a group of 18 entrepreneurs to work out of a rented Mexican mansion in Tulum. He called it Launch House. For a month, he and the crew posted  photos of themselves scuba diving, sharing meals and using their laptops at the beach.
 When a second group of entrepreneurs arrived in late October, Mr. Houck upgraded to a house with faster Wi-Fi, along with a private chef, a housekeeper and whiteboards in the pool for “poolstorms” — brainstorming sessions in the water.
 The goal, Mr. Houck said, was to help founders release new apps and software. “We’re not going out and partying in Tulum or going to the clubs,” he said. “We work from the beach, work from the house.”
 They couldn’t work from anywhere when a tropical storm and a hurricane cut the power. “Realistically, in places like Tulum, there’s always a chance the Wi-Fi will go out,” Mr. Houck said. “That’s a trade-off you make.”
 The much riskier trade-off, of course, is the pandemic. Three people in the first group, who Mr. Houck said had violated house rules and spent time with outsiders, got the virus. The second batch of entrepreneurs was told they would be evicted if they threatened the group’s safety.
 It is a reminder of the steep risks taken by the Covid Carpe Diem set. The reason this once-a-generation moment exists is the same reason most of us can’t go into the office or take a real vacation or eat inside a restaurant. Traveling risks sickness. Seizing the day risks sickness.
 In October, Brett Martin, a venture capital investor, had a heart-to-heart Zoom call with four friends — a Bitcoin trader, a writer, an entrepreneur and an architect. Until then, the group had been making plans to move to what they described as a “time-share commune” in Costa Rica from November to March.
 The rules had been set: They would buy health insurance through Costa Rica’s government. They would go on a “visa run” to Nicaragua halfway through to stamp their passports. No indoor dining. No work or conference calls from the main living area. Groceries would be delivered. Visitors would be allowed by group consent only.
 But one issue remained. “Getting really sick far away from home in a place where you don’t speak the language and being alone and helpless — that’s everyone’s fear,” Mr. Martin said.
 On the Zoom call, they decided: No man left behind. “If someone gets sick, this is like a family,” Mr. Martin said. (Just in case, he’s done two months of Duolingo.)
 He acknowledged that the setup could easily turn sour. Members of the group will have to adjust to having roommates again. The internet could go out. The price of Bitcoin could tank. He had to perfectly time a coronavirus test to get results 72 hours before flying. But after seven months of quarantine, he said, it was worth the risk.
 “The prospect of waking up at 6 in my studio apartment in New York, exercising, sitting down at my laptop for 10 to 12 hours, closing it, staring at the wall, then opening it again to watch Netflix for the rest of the night——” he trailed off. “It sounded literally dangerous to my health.”
 He got to Costa Rica in early November. It has poured rain every day. “Spirits,” he said, “are still high.”
title: 数字游牧民族没有为此做好准备
title_en: The Digital Nomads Did Not Prepare for This

它起作用了吗？是的，效果和广告上说的一模一样。你的iPhone将舒适地放在充电器的MagSafe一侧，使用内部磁铁进行对准。Apple Watch一侧向上和向外弹出，便于使用闭环式表带。整个设备可以折叠起来，便于携带，如果你不需要一侧或另一侧，甚至可以向后折叠。当然，这是可行的。
但这种折叠才是我们开始讨论可疑之处的地方。作为背景，你必须明白这个东西是129美元，但感觉它应该是70美元。当你意识到这是一款没有电源适配器的充电器时，如果你把它的价格降到40美元，我也不会感到震惊。充电器的盒子里有一根Lighting转USB-C数据线。这根线缆假定你有一个USB-C电源块，我认为这一点还不是普遍正确的。但充电器本身的闪电端口确实确保了你可以将其与任何现有的闪电充电电缆一起使用。
铰链和外壳上涂有柔软的橡胶，可以说是模压成型的。虽然铰链工作正常，但它会摇晃并立即起皱。橡胶足够厚，不会给人留下马上就会撕裂的印象--但也不能完全让人产生自信。这是一种廉价的铰链解决方案，你会期待从注重价格的第三方配件中看到这种解决方案，而不是苹果。
因为整个东西都是冲压成型的，所以还有一个围绕着单元外部的凸起。它上面有一个基本的封条，但如果你仔细看，你可以看到层叠的层。它看起来像是成熟了，磨损了，弯曲了，弄脏了。对于原本打算扔进袋子里的东西来说，这可不是什么好事。
怀特在那里也是个糟糕的选择，我的衣服已经变得很脏了，我现在哪儿也去不了。
这款手机充电速度足够快，设备也能很好地锁定。这款手表的充电部分拥有光滑的小镀铬铰链，给人的感觉是最高级的。MagSafe Pad和Watch充电器是苹果典型的喷丸铝，整个产品有相当大的份量。不管它的价值是什么，我无法测试一件事，那就是它是否能用20W电源适配器同时为苹果手表和快速充电的iPhone充电。我会试着用这些信息更新这篇评论。
当然，由于iPhone的侧面是有磁性的，如果它是连在一起的，你确实可以拿起手机的MagSafe好处，但当它是一大捆充电器时，比当它是手机背面一个纤细的MagSafe冰球时，这更令人尴尬。也许在你的酒店快速检查一下闹钟没问题。
但我很遗憾地说，在大肆炒作空中力量并最终消亡之后，我觉得整件事有点平淡无奇。苹果很可能在试图实现空中力量的整个过程中都计划好了这件事，但故事的弧线落在这款设备上确实是可悲的长号。
MagSafe二人组确实奏效了，而且还有几个工程上的亮点。但当你购买129美元的充电器和19美元的20W电源砖时，你会觉得它不值这么多钱，而且市场上有很多第三方配件可以很好地完成这项工作。
note_en:
Does it work? Yep, works exactly as advertised. Your iPhone will rest comfortably on the MagSafe side of the charger, aligning using the internal magnets. The Apple Watch side pops up and out to allow easy access for closed loop bands. The whole unit folds over to make it easier to travel with and will even fold over backwards if you don’t need one side or the other. It works, for sure.
   But that folding is where we start to get to the iffy stuff. For context, you have to understand that this thing is $129 but feels like it should be $70. When you realize that it is a charger that   doesn’t come with a power adapter , I would not be shocked if you mentally downgraded it to $40. The charger does come with a Lighting to USB-C cable in the box. That cable assumes, which I don’t think is at all universally true yet, that you have a USB-C power brick. But the Lightning port on the charger itself does ensure that you can use this with any existing Lightning charging cables,
  The hinge and casing are coated in soft touch rubber that is sort of press molded on. While the hinge works fine, it is wobbly and immediately creases. The rubber is thick enough that it doesn’t give the impression that it will rip immediately or anything — but it’s not exactly confidence inducing. This is an inexpensive hinge solution that you would expect to see from a price conscious third-party accessory, not from Apple.
  Because the whole thing is press molded, there is also this ridge that runs around the exterior of the unit. It has a basic seal on it but you can see the layers of lamination if you look closely. It looks ripe to nick, fray, bend and get dirty. Not great for something that’s meant to throw in a bag.
 White also a bad choice there, mine’s already getting dingy and I can’t even travel anywhere right now.
  The thing charges adequately fast and the devices lock on well. The Watch charging part of it feels the most premium with its smooth little chrome hinge. The MagSafe Pad and Watch charger are Apple’s typically nicely peened aluminum, and the whole thing has a decent amount of heft to it. For what it’s worth, I was unable to test one thing which is whether it will charge an Apple Watch and an iPhone in fast charge at the same time with the 20W power adapter. I’ll try to update this review with that info.
 Of course, because the iPhone side is magnetic, you do get the MagSafe benefit of being able to pick the phone up if it’s attached, but that’s more awkward when it’s a big bundle of charger vs when it’s a slim MagSafe puck on the back of your phone. Fine for a quick alarm check in your hotel maybe.
 But I’m sorry to say that I find the whole thing a bit underwhelming after the hype of AirPower and its eventual demise. Apple may very well have had this thing planned the whole time that it was trying to make AirPower happen, but the arc of that story landing on this device is sad trombone indeed.
 The MagSafe Duo does work, and there are a couple of engineering bright spots. But you will not feel that it’s worth the money by the time you purchase the $129 charger and the $19 20W power brick to go with it, and there are many third party accessories on the market that do this job just fine.
title: Apple MagSafe Duo Charger Review：有用，但昂贵，平淡无奇
title_en: Apple Magsafe Duo Charger Review: Useful, but expensive and underwhelming

Hello and welcome back to  Equity, TechCrunch’s venture capital-focused podcast where we unpack the numbers behind the headlines.
 This is Equity Monday, our weekly kickoff that tracks the latest big news, chats about the coming week, digs into some recent funding rounds and mulls over a larger theme or narrative from the private markets. 欢迎回到空间站，这是一份时事通讯，专门介绍现在和未来人们和包裹从A点到B点的所有方式。
随时给我发电子邮件至kirsten.korosec@techcrunch.com，与我分享想法、批评、提出意见或小贴士。你也可以直接发消息给我：Twitter-@kirsten korosec。
随着总统竞选愈演愈烈，全世界都在拭目以待，特朗普总统是否会留任，乔·拜登(Joe Biden)是否会成为美国第46任领导人，选举日变成了选举周。
周六上午，美联社、福克斯新闻和所有其他主要新闻媒体报道了这场竞选，提名乔·拜登为当选总统。计票工作仍将继续，并最终使各州的选举人团选举人按照我们的选举程序，在12月14日正式投票选举总统和副总统。
假设拜登宣誓就任下一任美国总统，交通很可能不会是他的第一个关注领域。然而，他在车祸中失去第一任妻子和女儿的个人经历、对气候变化的看法以及对克尔维特和美国铁路公司的爱将如何影响联邦交通政策，这将是一件有趣的事情。该国有深刻的基础设施需求，处于危机中的铁路服务，以及专注于自动化车辆技术商业化的新兴科技行业。
当然，选举日不仅仅是关于特朗普和拜登。美国各地的选票包含了数十项与交通相关的措施，包括公共交通资金，车主的维修权，零工经济工人应该被归类为雇员还是独立承包商。
加州的投票法案22号提案可能是最引人注目的竞选活动，这要归功于优步(Uber)、Lyft和其他依赖临时工的公司贡献了数千万美元，帮助获得支持并使其获得通过。选民们通过了第22号提案，这意味着零工工人将继续被归类为独立工人。使用零工的公司将被要求提供至少为最低工资的120%的收入保证，每工作英里30美分的费用，医疗津贴，工伤职业意外保险，免受歧视和性骚扰的保护，以及汽车事故和责任保险。
刚刚在选举日取得成功的优步表示，它将继续推动与22号提案类似的法律。这家叫车服务公司希望通过法律保护其商业模式的雄心是全球性的。优步首席执行官Dara Khosrowshahi周四在与分析师的财报电话会议上表示，该公司将“更大声地倡导像22号提案这样的法律。”他后来补充说，这将是该公司的优先事项，“与美国和世界各国政府合作，使之成为现实。”
加州、佐治亚州、马萨诸塞州、密歇根州、俄勒冈州和华盛顿州的选民还决定了其他与交通相关的措施。在19项与公共交通相关的措施中，有15项获得通过，两项失败，佐治亚州格温内特县的一项措施仍然“势均力敌”。交通卓越中心创建了一个方便的电子表格，跟踪2020年选举与公共交通相关的投票措施。
最后，马萨诸塞州的另一项吸引了大量关注和游说资金的投票措施是问题1。这项投票措施以75%的投票率通过，修改并扩大了一项法律，该法律赋予马萨诸塞州的消费者修理他们自己的汽车的权利。
从2022年开始，在马萨诸塞州销售装有远程信息处理系统的汽车的汽车制造商现在必须为它们配备一个标准化的开放数据平台。这个标准化的开放数据平台必须让车主和独立的维修机构能够直接访问，并能够通过基于移动的应用程序检索机械数据和运行诊断。
重要的是，这一措施涵盖了远程信息处理系统收集和无线传输的数据。它不仅可以访问机械数据，还允许车主和独立的机械师向车辆发送命令，进行维修、维护和诊断测试。
结果呢？虽然这项投票措施仅限于马萨诸塞州，但它将扩大到全国其他地区是有先例的。最初的修理权于2013年在马萨诸塞州生效。到2014年，该行业在一份谅解备忘录中同意扩大该法案的范围，覆盖美国其他地区。
交通不只是在不同地点之间移动，它还涉及停车等不太活跃的时刻。这让我们看到了本周一笔令人瞠目结舌的交易。
总部位于迈阿密的Reef Technology最初名为ParkJockey，从包括软银(Softbank)和穆巴达拉公司(Mubadala Corp.)在内的一群投资者那里获得了7亿美元的资金。
Reef为停车场提供硬件、软件和管理服务。但它最近增加了其他服务，比如为云厨房、医疗诊所、物流和最后一英里的送货提供基础设施，甚至是实体零售和体验式消费空间。
该公司联合创始人兼首席执行官阿里·奥贾尔沃(Ari Ojalvo)表示，这笔资金将用于在全国范围内从4800个地点扩大到1万个新地点，并将停车场改造成“社区中心”。私募股权和金融投资巨头橡树资本(Oaktree)、瑞银资产管理公司(UBS Asset Management)和欧洲风险投资公司塔吉特全球(Target Global)也参与了这轮融资。
正如TechCrunch的乔纳森·谢伯(Jonathan Shieber)在对Reef轮融资的报道中指出的那样，与WeWork一样，Reef租赁了其运营的大部分房地产，并对其进行升级，然后再将其出租给其他居住者(或使用这些空间本身)。与WeWork不同的是，该业务实际上有很好的发展机会-特别是考虑到为阻止新冠肺炎疫情蔓延而实施的健康和安全措施加速了商业趋势。
另一个激光雷达空间！AEVA是最新一家避开传统IPO路径，通过与一家特殊目的收购公司合并而上市的公司。这也是继Velodyne和Lumar之后，第三家采用这种方式进入公开市场的激光雷达公司。
AEVA是一家总部位于加利福尼亚州山景城的激光雷达公司，由两名前苹果工程师创立，并得到保时捷SE的支持。该公司宣布将与特殊目的收购公司InterPrivate Acquisition Corp.合并，交易后的市场估值为21亿美元。与InterPrivate的交易预计将在2021年初完成。
Logisly是一家总部位于雅加达的初创公司，自称是一个“B2B科技物流平台”。该公司今天宣布，它已经筹集了600万美元的首轮融资，以帮助简化印尼的物流。这轮融资由Monk‘s Hill Ventures牵头。
英国初创公司棉花糖(Marshmlow)在首轮融资中筹集了3000万美元。该公司旨在用一种新的风险确定方法与传统保险巨头展开竞争。该公司募集资金后的估值为3.1亿美元。
在加州和中国运营的自动驾驶汽车公司Pony.ai在新一轮注资2.67亿美元后，目前估值为53亿美元。这轮融资由安大略省教师养老金计划委员会(Ontario Teachers‘Payment Plan Board)旗下的创新基金TIP牵头，专注于对提供颠覆性技术的公司进行后期风险和成长性股权投资。现有合作伙伴富达中国特殊情况公司(Fidelity China Special Situations PLC)、5Y Capital(前身为晨兴风险投资)、ClearVue Partners和Eight Roads也参与了这轮融资。
Provizio开发了一款感官平台，称其能够实时并超出视线范围感知、预测和预防车祸。该公司完成了一轮620万美元的种子投资。Automatic Stuff的创始人、Movidius、欧洲创新理事会(EIC)和ACT风险投资的创始人鲍比·汉布里克(Bobby Hamrick)参与了这轮融资。
据The Information报道，Scale AI是一家初创公司，使用软件和人来处理和标记图像、激光雷达和地图数据，为丰田和Zoox等构建机器学习算法的公司提供服务。据The Information报道，该公司即将成为一家估值30亿美元的公司。据报道，这家由230岁的亚历山大·王(Alexandr Wang)创立并领导的公司收到了老虎全球管理公司(Tiger Global Management)的投资要约，对Scale的估值为32亿美元，是之前估值的三倍。
note_en:
Welcome back to The Station, a newsletter dedicated to all the present and future ways people and packages move from Point A to Point B.
   Email me anytime at  kirsten.korosec@techcrunch.com to share thoughts, criticisms, offer up opinions or tips. You can also send a direct message to me at Twitter —  @kirstenkorosec.
   Election Day turned into Election Week as the presidential race tightened and the world waited to see if President Trump would remain in office or if Joe Biden would become the 46th leader of the country.
 On Saturday morning, AP, Fox News and every other major news outlets called the race, naming Joe Biden president-elect. The ballot counts will still continue and eventually lead to each state’s Electoral College electors formally casting their votes for president and vice president on December 14, as dictated by our election process.
 Assuming Biden is sworn in as the next president of the United States, transportation will likely not be his first area of focus. However, it will be interesting to see how his personal experience of losing his first wife and daughter in a car crash, views on climate change and love for Corvettes as well as Amtrak might shape federal transportation policy. The country has deep infrastructure needs, a rail service in crisis and an emerging tech sector focused on commercializing automated vehicle technology.
 Election Day was, of course, about more than Trump and Biden.  Ballots throughout the U.S. contained dozens of transportation-related measures, including public transit funding, a car owner’s right to repair and whether gig economy workers should be classified as employees or independent contractors.
 Prop 22, the California ballot measure, might have been the most visible campaign thanks to the tens of millions of dollars that  Uber,   Lyft and other gig worker-reliant companies contributed to help garner support and get it passed. Voters approved Prop 22, which means that gig workers will continue to be classified as independent workers. Companies that use gig workers will be required to provide an earnings guarantee of at least 120% of minimum wage, 30 cents per engaged miles for expenses, a healthcare stipend, occupational accident insurance for on-the-job injuries, protection against discrimination and sexual harassment and automobile accident and liability insurance.
 Fresh off of its success on Election Day, Uber signaled that it will continue to push laws similar to the Prop 22. The ride-hailing company’s ambitions for laws that preserve its business model are global. Uber CEO Dara Khosrowshahi said Thursday during an earnings call with analysts that the company will  “more loudly advocate for laws like Prop 22.” He later added that it will be a priority of the company “to work with governments across the U.S. and the world to make this a reality.”
 There were at other transportation-related measures that were decided by voters in California, Georgia, Massachusetts, Michigan Oregon and Washington. Of the 19 measures related to public transit, 15 passed, two failed and one in Gwinnett County, Georgia is still “too close to call.” The Center for Transportation Excellence  created a handy spreadsheet tracking Election 2020 ballot measures related to public transit.
  Finally, another ballot measure, which received a lot of attention and lobbying dollars, was Question 1 in Massachusetts. The ballot measure, which was approved by 75% of voters, amends and broadens a law that gives consumers in Massachusetts the  right to repair the vehicles they own.
 Automakers that sell vehicles with telematics systems in Massachusetts will now have to equip them with a standardized open data platform beginning with model year 2022. This standardized open data platform has to give vehicle owners and independent repair facilities direct access and the ability to retrieve mechanical data and run diagnostics through a mobile-based application.
 Importantly, this measure covers the data that telematics systems collect and wirelessly transmit. And it not only gives access to the mechanical data, it allows owners and independent mechanics to send commands to the vehicle for repair, maintenance and diagnostic testing.
 The upshot? While this ballot measure is restricted to Massachusetts, there is precedent that it will expand to the rest of the country. The initial Right to Repair law went into effect in Massachusetts in 2013. By 2014, the industry agreed in a memorandum of understanding to expand that bill and cover the rest of the country.
   Transportation isn’t just about the movement between places; it’s also about the less active moments like parking. Which brings us to one eye-popping deal this week.
 REEF Technology, the Miami-based company that started its life as ParkJockey, raked in  $700 million from a group of investors that included Softbank and Mubadala Corp.
 REEF provides hardware, software and management services for parking lots. But it more recently added other services such as providing infrastructure for cloud kitchens, healthcare clinics, logistics and last-mile delivery and even brick-and-mortar retail and experiential consumer spaces.
 The company said that the money will be used to scale from 4,800 locations to 10,000 new locations around the country and to transform the parking lots into “neighborhood hubs,” according to Ari Ojalvo, the company’s co-founder and chief executive. Private equity and financial investment giants Oaktree, UBS Asset Management and the European venture capital firm Target Global also participated in the round.
 As TechCrunch’s Jonathan Shieber noted in his coverage of the REEF round, like WeWork, REEF leases most of the real estate it operates and upgrades it before leasing it to other occupants (or using the spaces itself). Unlike WeWork, the business actually has a fair shot at working out — especially given business trends that have accelerated in response to the health and safety measures implemented to stop the spread of the COVID-19 pandemic.
  ANOTHER LIDAR SPAC!  Aeva is the latest company to eschew the traditional IPO path and go public via a  merger with a special purpose acquisition company. It’s also the third lidar company, following Velodyne and Luminar, to take this route to the public markets.
 Aeva is a Mountain View, California-based lidar company started by two former Apple engineers and backed by Porsche SE. The company announced it was merging with special purpose acquisition company InterPrivate Acquisition Corp., with a post-deal market valuation of $2.1 billion. The deal with InterPrivate is expected to close by early 2021.
 Logisly, a Jakarta-based startup that describes itself as a “B2B tech-enabled logistics platform,” announced today it has raised  $6 million in Series A funding to help streamline logistics in Indonesia. The round was led by Monk’s Hill Ventures.
 Marshmallow, a UK startup aiming to take on legacy insurance giants with a new approach to determining risk,  raised $30 million in a Series A round. T he company has a post-fundraising valuation of $310 million.
 Pony.ai, the autonomous vehicle company that operates in California and China, is now  valued at $5.3 billion following a fresh injection of $267 million in funding. The round was led by TIP, an innovation fund within the Ontario Teachers’ Pension Plan Board that focuses on late-stage venture and growth equity investments in companies that deliver disruptive technology. Existing partners Fidelity China Special Situations PLC, 5Y Capital (formerly Morningside Venture Capital), ClearVue Partners and Eight Roads also participated in the round.
 Provizio, which developed a sensory platform it says can perceive, predict and prevent car accidents in real time and beyond the line-of-sight, closed a  seed investment round of $6.2 million. Bobby Hambrick, the founder of Autonomous Stuff, the founders of Movidius, the European Innovation Council (EIC) and ACT Venture Capital participated in the round.
 Scale AI, a startup that uses software and people to process and label image, lidar and map data for companies building machine learning algorithms such as Toyota and Zoox, is on the brink of becoming a company valued $3 billion,  The Information reported. The company founded and led by 230year-old Alexandr Wang reportedly received an offer of investment from Tiger Global Management valuing Scale at $3.2 billion pre-money, or triple its prior valuation.
title: 选票上的交通，软银把钱停在珊瑚礁，特斯拉龙舌兰酒来了
title_en: Transportation on the ballot, Softbank parks its money in REEF and Tesla Tequila arrives

印度监管机构印度竞争委员会周一宣布，将对谷歌提起反垄断诉讼。该委员会表示，将审查谷歌是否“不公平地”偏袒Play Store上的搜索结果，偏袒Google Pay应用程序；在安卓智能手机安装期间显著推广Google Pay；以及Play Store计费系统的设计是否“对双方(即通过UPI支付的应用程序)以及用户都不利”。
这位未透露姓名的线人声称，除了Google Play Store的计费系统支持Google Pay应用程序外，通过Play Store下载的应用程序的应用内购买也被授权支持Google Pay应用程序，“如果他们想要在Play Store上列出的话”。
谷歌对其功能应用程序列表进行了修改，如“编辑选择的应用程序”、“用户选择的应用程序”和“最受欢迎的免费应用程序”…。印度监管机构援引这位消息人士的话说：“这显示出明显偏向于自己的应用程序；操纵Play Store上的搜索广告算法，支持Google Pay；在安卓智能手机初始设置时预装并突出放置Google Pay，导致‘现状偏向’，不利于其他促进通过UPI支付的应用程序，”印度监管机构援引这名线人的话说。
“消息人士指出，苹果在欧洲的行为与谷歌在印度的行为有相似之处，即像苹果(如欧盟委员会新闻稿所述)，作为在Play Store上市的强制性要求，谷歌要求应用开发者只使用Google Play Store的支付系统和Google Play应用内计费，向在Play Store上购买应用或从应用内购买商品/服务(即IAP)的用户收费。此外，像苹果一样，谷歌向应用开发者收取30%的佣金，允许他们使用Play Store的支付系统和Google Play in-。“印度监管机构说。
如果这位举报人提供的指控可信，谷歌的做法可能违反了印度2002年竞争法第四节的各项规定。这位举报人的身份尚未确定。
Google Pay的前身是TEZ，是印度最受欢迎的支付服务之一。它与沃尔玛的PhonePe、Paytm和一系列其他应用程序展开竞争。截至上个月，沃尔玛的PhonePe在印度略微领先于谷歌支付(Google Pay)。这两款应用分别处理UPI上约40%的交易，UPI是由中国大型银行建设的支付基础设施。UPI是印度最受欢迎的数字支付解决方案。
Google Play商店支持一系列支付方式，包括信用卡、移动钱包、网上银行和UPI。但是，正如这位线人所说，“与其他数字支付解决方案相比，基于UPI的数字支付应用程序更方便、更安全、更经济。”印度监管机构表示，基于这些鲜明的特征，“举报人断言，通过UPI进行支付的应用市场是一个独立的相关市场，因为用户不认为通过UPI进行支付的应用可以与其他数字支付模式互换或替代。”
这起新的反垄断案是谷歌在印度的最新头疼之事。按用户计算，印度是谷歌最大的市场。根据研究公司Counterpoint的数据，近几个月来，安卓的主导地位也惹恼了中国的许多初创企业，它们已经组成了一个非正式的联盟来对抗这家安卓制造商。安卓在中国大约99%的智能手机上使用安卓。
note_en:
In its Monday  announcement (PDF) about opening an antitrust case against Google, Indian watchdog Competition Commission of India said it would review claims whether Google “unfairly” skews the search results on the Play Store in favor of Google Pay app over others; prominently promotes Google Pay during the setup of an Android smartphone; and if Play Store’s billing system is designed “to the disadvantage of both i.e. apps facilitating payment through UPI, as well as users.”
 The informant, who has not been identified, alleged that in addition to Google Play Store’s billing system favoring Google Pay app, in-app purchases for apps downloaded through Play Store are also mandated to support Google Pay app “if they want to be listed on the Play Store.”
  Google “rigs its feature app lists such as ‘Editor’s Choice Apps’, ‘User Choice Apps’ and ‘Top Free apps’ … demonstrating clear bias in favor of its own app; by manipulating the search advertisements algorithm on the Play Store in favour of Google Pay; and by pre-installing and prominently placing Google Pay on Android smartphones at the time of initial set-up resulting in a ‘status-quo bias’ to the detriment of other apps facilitating payments through UPI as well,” the informant was quoted as saying by the Indian watchdog.
 “The informant pointed out similarities between the conducts of Apple in Europe and Google in India i.e. like Apple (as set out in the Press Release of EC), as a mandatory requirement for listing on the Play Store, Google requires the app developers to exclusively use Google Play Store’s payment system and Google Play In-App Billing for charging users who purchase apps on the Play Store or buy goods/services from inside an app (i.e. IAP), and further like Apple, Google charges app developers a 30% commission for allowing them to use the Play Store’s payment system and Google Play In-App Billing,” the Indian watchdog said.
 If the allegations provided by the informant, who has not been identified, is found credible, Google’s practices could be in violation of various provisions of Section 4 of India’s Competition Act of 2002.
 Google Pay,  formerly known as Tez, is  one of the most popular payments services in India. It competes with Walmart’s PhonePe, Paytm, and a range of other apps. As of last month, Walmart’s  PhonePe   was slightly ahead of Google Pay in India. Both the apps individually process roughly 40% of all transactions on UPI, a payments infrastructure built by large banks in the country. UPI is the most popular digital payments solution in India.
 Google Play Store supports a range of payments methods, including credit cards, mobile wallets, internet banking, and UPI. But, as the informant alleges, “UPI based digital payment apps are more convenient, secured, economical, etc. over other digital payment solutions.” Based on such distinct features, the Indian watchdog said, “the Informant has averred that the market for apps facilitating payment through UPI is a separate relevant market as users do not regard apps facilitating payment through UPI as interchangeable or substitutable with other modes of digital payment.”
 The new antitrust case is the latest headache for Google in India, its biggest market by users. In recent months, the dominant position of Android — which powers roughly 99% smartphones in the country, according to research firm Counterpoint, has also irked many startups in the country, who have  formed an informal coalition to fight back the Android maker.
title: 印度反垄断监管机构表示，已对谷歌立案调查Play Store是否偏袒谷歌支付
title_en: India's antitrust watchdog says it has opened a case against Google to investigate whether Play Store favors Google Pay over rivals

以下是事件的完整时间线。从我第一次拒绝去美国旅行，直到我最终拿到旅游签证。然后我就不能去了。
我在夏威夷和莫兹拉--我当时的雇主--待了一周。这是我19年来第12次访问美国。我是通过ESTA(瑞典公民可以使用的免签计划)去那里的。我用过很多次了，这次没什么特别的。ESTA的典型程序是我们在网上申请：填写一张表格，支付14美元的费用，并在几天内得到确认，我们可以去了。
一天清晨，在瑞典阿兰达机场的值机柜台前，我被拒绝登机。完全出乎意料，出乎意料！我原以为我要乘坐英国航空公司(British Airways)的航班经由伦敦前往旧金山，但结果我不得不掉头回家--有点震惊。据柜台后面的女士说，“我的ESTA出了点问题”。我使用的ESTA和护照和我在2016年12月使用的一样好。它们的有效期是两年，而且还没有过期。
Mozilla请来帮助我们的人找不出问题的症结所在，也找不到答案(美国和瑞典都在尝试提问和调查)，所以我们把希望寄托在某个地方是人为的错误上，并决定下次再试一次。
由于其他原因，我错过了接下来的会议(2017年12月)，但在2018年夏天，另一场Mozilla全体会议即将到来(这次是在美国德克萨斯州)，所以我在活动之前及时申请了新的ESTA-因为我有点担心会出现问题。我是对的，我很快就被拒绝了ESTA。“未经授权旅行”。
啊哈。这意味着去年没有搞错，他们实际上是这个意思。我改变了做法，转而申请了旅游签证。我花了160美元，填写了关于我和我过去15年旅行的大量荒谬的信息，我还去了美国大使馆进行了面谈和指纹采集。
这是签证程序的第0天，在我第一次在阿兰达停留296天后。
我没有及时拿到签证，错过了旧金山的全体会议。
我退出了Mozilla，所以我没有更多的理由去他们的公司All-Hands…。
一年过去了。当我问到进展情况时，大使馆的电子邮件人员声称：“有人正在做这件事。”
我们会以最快捷的方式处理所有申请。虽然我们理解你的沮丧，但我们必须遵守移民法关于签证发放的规定。这一过程无法加快或绕过。请放心，我们会在行政处理结束后尽快与您联系。
又过去了一年，我已经放弃了所有的希望。现在，它变成了一个博彩游戏和科学项目。他们可以在不说是或不是的情况下将这一过程拖延多久？
我的一位美国公民朋友联系了他的国会议员格里·康诺利(Gerry Connolly)，询问我的情况并寻求帮助。他的办公室随后向美国驻斯德哥尔摩大使馆提出问题，询问我的情况。而9月17日收到的回复是相当负面的…。
你的案件目前正在进行必要的行政处理，令人遗憾的是，无法预测这一处理将于何时完成。
…。我认为接下来的事态发展表明它产生了影响。它打通了什么东西。
在我与大使馆面谈889天后(仅在回答国会议员的5天后)，大使馆通过电子邮件与我联系。这是自2018年4月那天以来的第一次。
您的签证申请仍在行政处理中。然而，我们遗憾地通知您，由于您错过了旅行计划，我们将要求您更新旅行计划。
我的旅行计划--过去800天左右已经过时了--突然需要更新了！由于我已经在这个过程中进行了很长时间，而且我担心现在放弃会迫使我回到起点，如果我现在停下来，以后再试一次的话，我决定为自己安排一些最新的旅行计划。毕竟，我在一家美国公司工作，我在那里有一两个朋友。
我回复了询问旅行计划细节的电话，并附上了一封官方邀请信，邀请我去拜访由我们的首席执行官拉里签名的沃尔夫斯勒公司(WolfSSL)的同事。我真的很想在某个时候做这件事，因为我从来没有见过他们中的大多数人，所以这不是一个编造的原因。我甚至可以让其他一些朋友邀请我来推动这一过程，但我认为这个邀请应该足以让事情继续进行下去。
我又收到一封电子邮件。现在距离采访已经910天了。大使馆要求“进一步处理”我的护照。
我把护照寄给了斯德哥尔摩的美国大使馆。我还按照指示订购并支付了“退货邮资”，这样他们就能以安全的方式寄回给我。
早上10点半，我的手机亮了，显示了一条短信，告诉我有一个从“美国大使馆”寄给我的包裹(打字错误会加分)。
我拿到了我的护照。里面有一张有效期为10年的美国签证，有效期至2030年11月。
作为额外的好处，签证还附带了NIE(国家利益例外)，允许我在PP(总统声明)期间单次进入美国-这限制了从欧洲申根区到美国的旅行。换句话说：我实际上是被允许马上去旅行的！
这一时机令人着迷。我上一次在美国时，特朗普还没有就职，在拜登被宣布为下一任美国总统几天后，我就拿到了批准的签证。
新冠肺炎还在我们头上，大流行看不到尽头。当然，我不会去美国或任何其他国家旅行，直到它被认为是安全和明智的。
当疫情得到控制，旅行变得可行时，我相信会有机会。希望在签证到期之前，情况会有所改善。
我在美国和其他地方的所有家人和朋友，在整个过程中都支持我，让我振作起来。谢谢你一直邀请我去美国参加有趣的活动，尽管我一直没能参加。感谢您推动在美国境外组织活动！很抱歉，我错过了社交聚会，错过了朋友的婚礼，错过了几次会议演讲的机会。感谢在这漫长的疯狂旅程中所给予的所有精神支持。
特别感谢大卫(你知道你是谁)联系了格里·康诺利的办公室。老实说，我认为这是最终推动这一进程的关键事件。
note_en:
Here’s the complete timeline of events. From my first denial to travel to the US until I eventually received a tourist visa. And then I can’t go anyway.
  I spent a week on Hawaii with Mozilla – my employer at the time. This was my 12th visit to the US over a period of 19 years. I went there on ESTA, the visa waiver program Swedish citizens can use. I’ve used it many times, there was nothing special this time. The typical procedure with ESTA is that we apply online: fill in a form, pay a 14 USD fee and get a confirmation within a few days that we’re good to go.
   In the early morning one day by the check-in counter at Arlanda airport in Sweden, I was refused to board my flight. Completely unexpected and out of the blue! I thought I was going to San Francisco via London with British Airways, but instead I had to turn around and go back home – slightly shocked. According to the lady behind the counter there was “something wrong with my ESTA”. I used the same ESTA and passport as I used just fine back in December 2016. They’re made to last two years and it had not expired.
  People engaged by Mozilla to help us out could not figure out or get answers about what the problem was (questions and investigations were attempted both in the US and in Sweden), so we put our hopes on that it was a human mistake somewhere and decided to just try again next time.
  I missed the following meeting (in December 2017) for other reasons but in the summer of 2018 another Mozilla all-hands meeting was coming up (in Texas, USA this time) so I went ahead and applied for a new ESTA in good time before the event – as I was a bit afraid there was going to be problems. I was right and I got denied ESTA very quickly. “Travel Not Authorized”.
   Gaaah. It meant it was no mistake last year, they actually mean this. I switched approach and instead applied for a tourist visa. I paid 160 USD, filled in a ridiculous amount of information about me and my past travels over the last 15 years and I visited the US embassy for an in-person interview and fingerprinting.
  This is day 0 in the  visa process, 296 days after I was first stopped at Arlanda.
  I missed the all-hands meeting in San Francisco when I didn’t get the visa in time.
  I  quit Mozilla, so I then had no more reasons to go to their company all-hands…
  A year passed. “someone is working on it” the embassy email person claimed when I asked about progress.
     All applications are processed in the most expeditious manner possible. While we understand your frustration, we are required to follow immigration law regarding visa issuances. This process cannot be expedited or circumvented. Rest assured that we will contact you as soon as the administrative processing is concluded.
  Another year had passed and I had given up all hope. Now it turned into a betting game and science project. How long can they actually drag out this process without saying either yes or no?
  A friend of mine, a US citizen, contacted his Congressman –  Gerry Connolly – about my situation and asked for help. His office then subsequently sent a question to the US embassy in Stockholm asking about my case. While the response that arrived on September 17 was rather negative…
 your case is currently undergoing necessary administrative processing and regrettably it is not possible to predict when this processing will be completed.
 … I think the following turn of events indicates it had an effect. It unclogged something.
  After 889 days since my interview on the embassy (only five days after the answer to the congressman), the embassy contacted me over email.   For the first time since that April day in 2018.
 Your visa application is still in administrative processing. However, we regret to inform you that because you have missed your travel plans, we will require updated travel plans from you.
 My travel plans – that had been out of date for the last 800 days or so – suddenly needed to be updated! As I was already so long into this process and since I feared that giving up now would force me back to square one if I would stop now and re-attempt this again at a later time, I decided to arrange myself some updated travel plans. After all, I work for an American company and I have a friend or two there.
  I replied to the call for travel plan details with an official invitation letter attached, inviting me to go visit my colleagues at  wolfSSL signed by our CEO, Larry. I really want to do this at some point, as I’ve never met most of them so it wasn’t a made up reason. I could possibly even get some other friends to invite me to get the process going but I figured this invite should be enough to keep the ball rolling.
  I got another email. Now at 910 days since the interview. The embassy asked for my passport “for further processing”.
  I posted my passport to the US embassy in Stockholm. I also ordered and paid for “return postage” as instructed so that they would ship it back to me in a safe way.
  At 10:30 in the morning my phone lit up and showed me a text telling me that there’s an incoming parcel being delivered to me, shipped from “the Embassy of the United State” (bonus points for the typo).
   I received my passport. Inside, there’s a US visa that is valid for ten years, until November 2030.
  As a bonus, the visa also comes with a NIE (National Interest Exception) that allows me a single entry to the US during the PP (Presidential Proclamations) – which is restricting travels to the US from the European Schengen zone. In other words: I am actually allowed to travel right away!
 The timing is fascinating. The last time I was in the US, Trump hadn’t taken office yet and I get the approved visa in my hands just days after Biden has been announced as the next president of the US.
  Covid-19 is still over us and there’s no end in sight of the pandemic. I will of course not travel to the US or any other country until it can be deemed safe and sensible.
 When the pandemic is under control and traveling becomes viable, I am sure there will be opportunities. Hopefully the situation will improve before the visa expires.
  All my family and friends, in the US and elsewhere who have supported me and cheered me up through this entire process. Thanks for keeping inviting me to fun things in the US even though I’ve not been able to participate. Thanks for pushing for events to get organized outside of the US! I’m sorry I’ve missed social gatherings, a friend’s marriage and several conference speaking opportunities. Thanks for all the moral support throughout this long journey of madness.
 A special thanks go to David (you know who you are) for contacting Gerry Connolly’s office. I honestly think this was the key event that finally made things move in this process.
title: 937天后拿到美国签证
